perturbation.original_text: Each party agrees that it shall protect the confidentiality of, and take reasonable steps to prevent disclosure or unauthorized use of, the Confidential Information in order to prevent it from falling into the public domain or the possession of persons not legally bound to maintain its confid entiality, provided that in no event shall such party's obligations exceed the reasonable standard of care taken to protect its own confidential information of like importance.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party agrees that it shall protect the confidentiality of, and take reasonable steps to prevent disclosure or unauthorized use of, the Confidential Information only when explicitly requested by the disclosing party, and only to the extent that such steps do not significantly impede ordinary business operations.
perturbation.explanation: The original text in Section 2 mandates 'reasonable steps' to protect Confidential Information, whereas the modified text limits this obligation to 'when explicitly requested' and only if it 'do[es] not significantly impede ordinary business operations.' This contradicts the original spirit of the agreement to protect confidential information at all times as described in Section 1. This change allows a party to potentially disclose confidential information if protecting it is deemed too cumbersome.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidential Information may include confidential and proprietary information which is disclosed by one party to the other party and is labeled as confidential or proprietary; however, labeling is not required for information to be considered confidential.
perturbation.justification: The modified text in Section 2 significantly weakens the protection of Confidential Information by limiting it to only when explicitly requested and only if it doesn't impede business operations. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that labeling is not required for information to be considered confidential. The modified text implies that if information is not labeled confidential or a request is not made, it may not be protected, even if it is confidential. The original text mandates 'reasonable steps' to protect Confidential Information, while the modified text introduces conditions that could allow for disclosure, creating a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each party acknowledges that its obligations hereunder survive in accordance with the terms hereof, notwithstanding the termination of the business relationship of the parties, for a period of two (2) years following the last disclo sure of Confidential Information by the other party hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party acknowledges that its obligations hereunder survive in accordance with the terms hereof, notwithstanding the termination of the business relationship of the parties, for a period of one (1) year following the termination of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause specifies obligations surviving 'for a period of two (2) years following the last disclosure,' whereas the modified clause changes this to 'one (1) year following the termination of this agreement.' This contradiction creates uncertainty about the duration of confidentiality obligations, especially if confidential information is disclosed near the end of the agreement's term. Now the survival clause is dependent on the termination of the agreement which creates a contradiction with all the previous clauses.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidential Information may include confidential and proprietary information which is disclosed by one party to the other party and is labeled as confidential or proprietary; however, labeling is not required for information to be considered confidential.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies a survival period of two years following the last disclosure of confidential information, while the changed text shortens this to one year following the termination of the agreement. The contradicted text states that confidential information can be considered confidential even if not labeled, which relies on the 'last disclosure' timeframe to determine the obligations. Because the changed text alters the timeframe and now makes it based on the termination of the agreement, a contradiction is created and creates ambiguity about the duration of confidentiality obligations, especially if confidential information is disclosed near the end of the agreement's term. This fits the 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction' definition as the obligation is defined inconsistently.

perturbation.original_text: Any materials, documents, notes, memoranda, software code, drawings, sketches and other tangible items containing, consisting of or relating to the Confidential Information of a party which are furnished hereunder to the other party, or are in the possession of the other party, remain the property of the party which disclosed the Confidential Information and shall be promptly returned to such party upon the disclosing party’s request therefore.
perturbation.changed_text: Any materials, documents, notes, memoranda, software code, drawings, sketches and other tangible items containing, consisting of or relating to the Confidential Information of a party which are furnished hereunder to the other party, or are in the possession of the other party, may be retained by the receiving party for archival purposes after the termination of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original Section 3 mandates that all materials related to Confidential Information 'shall be promptly returned' upon request. The modified text permits the receiving party to retain these materials 'for archival purposes after the termination of this agreement.' This change allows the receiving party to keep confidential information even after the agreement ends, directly contradicting the return obligation. The change contradicts Section 1 which refers to Confidential Information, and it implies the return of all Confidential Information.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidential Information may include confidential and proprietary information which is disclosed by one party to the other party and is labeled as confidential or proprietary; however, labeling is not required for information to be considered confidential.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated the prompt return of all confidential information related materials upon request, whereas the changed text permits the receiving party to retain these materials after termination of the agreement for archival purposes. This directly contradicts the obligation to return all confidential information, creating an in-text contradiction. Section 1 defines Confidential Information and the modified text now allows retention of that information, contradicting the implicit return requirement by keeping them for archival purposes after termination.

perturbation.original_text: Each party's obligations under this Agreement shall not apply to information which: (a) is known by the receiving party or is publicly available at the time of disclosure; (b) becomes publicly available after disclosure by the disclosing party to the receiving party through no act of either party; (c) is hereafter rightfully furnished to the receiving party by a third party without restriction as to use or disclosure; (d) is disclosed with the prior written consent of the disclosing party; (e) is information that was independently developed by the receiving party; or (f) is required to be disclosed pursuant to any judicial or administrative proceeding, provided that the receiving party promptly notifies the disclosing party of such action and gives the disclosing party the opportunity to seek any legal remedies to maintain such information in confidence.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party's obligations under this Agreement shall not apply to information which: (a) is known by the receiving party or is publicly available at the time of disclosure; (b) becomes publicly available after disclosure by the disclosing party to the receiving party through no act of either party; (c) is hereafter rightfully furnished to the receiving party by a third party without restriction as to use or disclosure; (d) is disclosed with the prior written consent of the disclosing party; (e) is information that was independently developed by the receiving party; or (f) is required to be disclosed pursuant to any judicial or administrative proceeding, provided that the receiving party is not required to promptly notify the disclosing party of such action nor give the disclosing party the opportunity to seek any legal remedies to maintain such information in confidence.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires the receiving party to 'promptly notif[y]' the disclosing party and allow them to 'seek any legal remedies' if confidential information must be disclosed in a legal proceeding. The modified version eliminates this notification and opportunity. This limits the disclosing party’s ability to protect their information and contradicts the intent of maintaining confidentiality established in other sections, mainly Section 1. The modified clause potentially exposes confidential information without allowing the disclosing party a chance to intervene.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Confidential Information may include confidential and proprietary information which is disclosed by one party to the other party and is labeled as confidential or proprietary; however, labeling is not required for information to be considered confidential.
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement for the receiving party to notify the disclosing party and allow them to seek legal remedies if confidential information is disclosed in a legal proceeding. This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which states that Confidential Information may include information disclosed by one party to the other. By removing the notification requirement, the changed text undermines the ability of the disclosing party to protect their confidential information, creating a strong in-text contradiction related to confidentiality, a type of Omissions – In Text Contradiction. The change limits the disclosing party’s ability to protect their information and contradicts the intent of maintaining confidentiality established in the contradicted section. The modified clause potentially exposes confidential information without allowing the disclosing party a chance to intervene.
